seo.imageAlt: A steaming bowl of authentic Chinese Hot and Sour Soup garnished with green onions and chili oil.

description: Experience the ultimate comfort with our authentic Hot and Sour Soup, a classic Chinese dish renowned for its perfect balance of fiery spice and tangy vinegar. This hearty soup is packed with tender pork, silken tofu, and crisp vegetables, all swimming in a rich, savory broth. It is a warming and satisfying meal, perfect for any occasion.

ingredients.notes: mixed with 3 tbsp cold water to form a slurry

instructions.title: Prepare Ingredients
instructions.text: Begin by preparing all your ingredients. Slice pork, dice tofu, rehydrate and slice mushrooms, and julienne bamboo shoots. Whisk eggs and prepare the cornstarch slurry.

instructions.title: Cook Pork and Vegetables
instructions.text: In a large pot or Dutch oven, heat a small amount of oil over medium-high heat. Add the sliced pork and stir-fry until it is no longer pink, about 2-3 minutes. Add the sliced shiitake mushrooms, wood ear mushrooms, and bamboo shoots. Stir-fry for another 2 minutes.

instructions.title: Add Broth and Simmer
instructions.text: Pour in the chicken or vegetable broth. Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce the heat to medium-low and let it simmer for 10 minutes to allow the flavors to meld. Add the diced tofu.

instructions.title: Thicken the Soup
instructions.text: Stir in the soy sauce, rice vinegar, and white pepper. Give the cornstarch slurry a quick stir and slowly pour it into the simmering soup while continuously stirring. Continue to stir until the soup thickens to your desired consistency, about 1-2 minutes.

instructions.title: Add Eggs
instructions.text: Once the soup has thickened, slowly drizzle the beaten eggs into the hot soup in a thin stream while stirring gently in one direction. This will create beautiful egg ribbons. Cook for about 1 minute until the egg is set.

instructions.title: Finish and Serve
instructions.text: Remove the soup from the heat. Stir in the sesame oil. Taste and adjust seasonings if necessary, adding more vinegar or white pepper to your preference. Ladle the hot soup into bowls, garnish with sliced green onions, and drizzle with chili oil for an extra kick.

tips: For authentic flavor, use fresh white pepper and adjust to your spice preference; it is crucial for the 'hot' element.
Do not skip the cornstarch slurry and beaten egg steps; they give the soup its signature silky texture and visual appeal.
Hot and Sour Soup is best served immediately. If reheating, add a splash of broth or water to thin if needed, and re-check seasonings.
article:
Embark on a culinary journey to the heart of Chinese cuisine with a bowl of Hot and Sour Soup. This iconic dish, celebrated for its complex interplay of flavors and comforting warmth, has captivated palates around the globe. Far from a simple broth, it's a symphony of spicy, tangy, savory, and umami notes, creating an experience that is both invigorating and deeply satisfying. Whether you're a seasoned chef or a curious home cook, mastering this classic Chinese soup is a rewarding endeavor that promises to impress.

## What is Hot and Sour Soup?

Hot and Sour Soup is a quintessential Chinese appetizer or light meal, renowned for its distinctive flavor profile. Originating from Sichuan province, though popular throughout China with regional variations, its name perfectly describes its essence: a bold tanginess from vinegar and a fiery kick from white pepper, often complemented by chili oil. Beyond the primary hot and sour elements, the soup boasts a rich umami depth from ingredients like soy sauce, mushrooms, and broth, balanced by a savory touch. Texturally, it's a marvel, usually thickened to a pleasant consistency and filled with a medley of finely shredded ingredients such as pork, bamboo shoots, and various types of mushrooms, all bound together by delicate strands of egg. It’s a hearty, warming, and stimulating soup that awakens the senses and prepares the palate for further culinary delights.

## Ingredients You Need

To create an authentic and delicious Hot and Sour Soup at home, gather the following ingredients. Quality ingredients are key to unlocking the true potential of this flavorful dish.

For the Base:
*   6 cups chicken or vegetable broth (low sodium preferred)
*   2 tablespoons soy sauce (or tamari for gluten-free)
*   3-4 tablespoons rice vinegar (adjust to taste for desired sourness)
*   1-2 tablespoons white pepper (start with 1, add more for heat)
*   1 tablespoon sesame oil
*   1 teaspoon dark soy sauce (optional, for richer color)
*   Salt to taste

For the Solids:
*   4-6 ounces lean pork loin or tenderloin, thinly shredded (or chicken breast, or firm tofu for vegetarian)
*   8-10 dried shiitake mushrooms, rehydrated, stems removed, thinly sliced
*   1/2 cup wood ear mushrooms, rehydrated, thinly sliced (available dried in Asian markets)
*   1 (8-ounce) can bamboo shoots, drained and thinly shredded
*   1/4 cup lily buds (optional, rehydrated and tied into knots, for traditional texture)

For Thickening and Finishing:
*   3 tablespoons cornstarch
*   1/4 cup cold water (for cornstarch slurry)
*   2 large eggs, whisked
*   2 green onions, thinly sliced (for garnish)
*   Fresh cilantro, chopped (for garnish, optional)
*   Chili oil (for serving, optional, for extra heat)

## Step-by-Step Instructions

Crafting your own Hot and Sour Soup is a straightforward process that yields incredibly rewarding results. Follow these steps for a perfect bowl:

1.  **Prepare Ingredients:** If using dried shiitake and wood ear mushrooms, place them in separate bowls, cover with hot water, and let them rehydrate for at least 30 minutes (or according to package directions). Once soft, squeeze out excess water, remove shiitake stems, and thinly slice both types of mushrooms. Shred the pork (or chicken/tofu), bamboo shoots, and lily buds (if using). Prepare your cornstarch slurry by whisking cornstarch with cold water until smooth. Whisk the eggs in a small bowl.
2.  **Cook Protein:** In a large pot or Dutch oven, bring the broth to a simmer over medium heat. Add the shredded pork and cook for 2-3 minutes until no longer pink. If using tofu, you can add it directly with the other solids.
3.  **Add Solids & Seasonings:** Stir in the rehydrated shiitake mushrooms, wood ear mushrooms, bamboo shoots, and lily buds (if using). Add soy sauce, dark soy sauce (if using), rice vinegar, and white pepper. Bring the soup back to a gentle simmer and let it cook for 5-7 minutes to allow the flavors to meld. Taste and adjust seasonings – you might want more vinegar for tang or more white pepper for heat.
4.  **Thicken the Soup:** Give the cornstarch slurry a quick re-whisk. While the soup is at a gentle simmer, slowly pour in the slurry in a thin stream, stirring constantly, until the soup thickens to your desired consistency. You might not need all of it.
5.  **Add Egg Drop:** Once the soup is thickened, reduce the heat to low. Slowly drizzle the whisked eggs into the simmering soup in a circular motion. Let the egg cook for about 30 seconds without stirring, then gently stir once or twice to create delicate strands of egg.
6.  **Finish and Serve:** Remove the pot from the heat. Stir in the sesame oil. Ladle the Hot and Sour Soup into individual bowls. Garnish generously with sliced green onions and fresh cilantro. Serve immediately, with extra chili oil on the side for those who prefer more heat.

## Pro Tips for the Best Results

Achieving that perfect balance of hot, sour, and savory in your Hot and Sour Soup requires a few insider tricks:

*   **Balance is Key:** The beauty of Hot and Sour Soup lies in its equilibrium. Don't be afraid to taste and adjust the vinegar and white pepper throughout the cooking process. Start with the recommended amounts and incrementally add more until it suits your preference. Some prefer more sour, others more spicy.
*   **Quality Mushrooms:** Dried shiitake and wood ear mushrooms are crucial for authentic flavor and texture. Rehydrate them properly; the soaking liquid from shiitake mushrooms can also be strained and added to the broth for extra umami depth.
*   **Cornstarch Slurry Savvy:** Always mix your cornstarch with cold water before adding it to hot liquid to prevent lumps. Add it slowly, stirring continuously, until you reach your desired thickness. Remember, the soup will thicken slightly more as it cools.
*   **White Pepper Power:** White pepper provides a distinct, pungent heat that is characteristic of this soup. While black pepper can be used in a pinch, it won't deliver the same authentic flavor profile.
*   **Egg Drop Technique:** For beautiful, delicate egg ribbons, pour the whisked egg slowly into the gently simmering soup from a slight height, in a circular motion. Let it set for a moment before gently stirring. Avoid vigorous stirring, which can break up the strands.
*   **Fresh Garnish:** Don't skip the fresh green onions and cilantro. They add a vibrant freshness and aromatic lift that completes the dish.

## Serving Suggestions

Hot and Sour Soup is incredibly versatile and can be enjoyed in various ways. Traditionally, it's served as an appetizer, warming the palate and stimulating the appetite before a larger Chinese meal.

*   **As an Appetizer:** Its bold flavors make it an excellent starter for any Chinese feast, pairing wonderfully with dishes like spring rolls, potstickers, or crispy wontons.
*   **With Rice or Noodles:** For a light lunch or dinner, serve a generous bowl of Hot and Sour Soup alongside a simple bowl of steamed white rice or plain noodles. The soup’s rich flavor acts as a delicious sauce for the grains.
*   **Part of a Dim Sum Spread:** It fits perfectly into a larger dim sum spread, offering a comforting liquid element amidst an array of small plates.
*   **Comfort Food:** On a chilly day, a bowl of this comforting soup can be a meal in itself, providing warmth and satisfaction.

## Variations and Substitutions

The beauty of Hot and Sour Soup lies in its adaptability. Feel free to experiment with these variations to suit your dietary needs or personal preferences:

*   **Vegetarian/Vegan Hot and Sour Soup:** Easily made vegetarian by omitting the pork and using vegetable broth. For a vegan version, also omit the egg (you can substitute with silken tofu blended into a creamy consistency for texture, though it won't create "egg drops"). Add more firm tofu or extra mushrooms for substance.
*   **Different Proteins:** Instead of pork, you can use shredded chicken breast, shrimp, or even thin slices of beef. Add shrimp towards the end of cooking as they cook quickly.
*   **Extra Vegetables:** Feel free to incorporate other thinly sliced vegetables like carrots, rehydrated cloud ear mushrooms, or even a handful of spinach or bean sprouts added at the very end for a fresh crunch.
*   **Spice Level:** If you crave more heat, beyond white pepper, add a dash of chili oil or a few thinly sliced fresh chilies (like bird's eye chilies) to the soup base.
*   **Gluten-Free:** Ensure you use tamari instead of regular soy sauce and verify that your broth and any other sauces are gluten-free.
*   **Darker Hue:** For a darker soup color, increase the amount of dark soy sauce. This primarily affects appearance, not flavor significantly.

Making your own Hot and Sour Soup from scratch is a deeply rewarding culinary experience. With its invigorating blend of flavors and textures, it's a dish that truly embodies the vibrant spirit of Chinese cuisine. So, gather your ingredients, follow these steps, and prepare to delight your taste buds with a homemade bowl of this enduring classic.
